concepts.title: Nomenclature Rules
concepts.description: For aldehydes, the prefix is derived from the parent chain and the suffix is -al. For ketones, the parent chain has the suffix -an. For carboxylic acids, the suffix is -oic acid.

concepts.title: Preparation of Aldehydes
concepts.description: Aldehydes can be prepared by the oxidation of primary alcohols using mild oxidizing agents like Tollens' reagent, Benedict's reagent, or by the reduction of nitriles.

concepts.title: Physical Properties of Ketones
concepts.description: Ketones are colourless liquids or solids with a characteristic pungent odour. They are soluble in water and have a higher boiling point than alkanes of similar molecular mass.

concepts.title: IUPAC Nomenclature
concepts.description: Mnemonic: 'Aunt Carrie Always Arranges Lovely Knick-Knacks' - A for Aldehyde, C for Carbonyl, A for Acid, L for Longer chain, K for Ketone, etc.

concepts.title: Oxidation of Alcohols
concepts.description: Students often confuse the oxidation of primary and secondary alcohols. Primary alcohols are oxidized to aldehydes and then to carboxylic acids, while secondary alcohols are oxidized to ketones.

concepts.title: Functional Group Tests
concepts.description: Tollens' test: +ve for aldehydes, -ve for ketones. Benedict's test: +ve for aldehydes and ketones. Lucas test: -ve for tertiary alcohols, +ve for primary and secondary alcohols.

concepts.title: Reaction with Tollen's Reagent
concepts.description: Aldehydes give a silver mirror with Tollen's reagent, while ketones do not.

concepts.title: Preparation of Ketones
concepts.description: Ketones can be prepared by the reaction of alcohols with ketone chlorides or by the oxidation of secondary alcohols.
